name: Chocolate Panna Cotta
intro: I still remember marveling at the silken texture of the first panna cotta (“cooked cream” in Italian) I ate in Italy more than twenty-five years ago. Ever since, I have been meaning to create a vegan version that eliminates the heavy cream or half-and-half and gelatin of traditional versions, but without too many flavors to interfere with the delicate texture. After much experimentation, I’m delighted with the results.

ingredients.item: canned unsweetened full-fat organic coconut milk, well-stirred (do not use light)
/ 18 grams organic whole cane sugar, ground in a blender until powdered
pure maple syrup, Grade B or dark amber, or agave syrup
/ 14 grams dark chocolate (66 to 70%), finely chopped
fresh berries or any other seasonal fresh fruit, for serving
to ⅔ cup / 120 to 160 ml All-Purpose Chocolate Syrup (page 274), for serving

method: Set four 3-ounce / 89-ml ramekins on a tray or small baking sheet.

Measure the agar into a medium saucepan. Pour the water over the agar, but do not stir or heat. Set aside for 15 minutes or longer to allow the agar to soften. This will help the agar dissolve thoroughly and easily.

Bring the liquid to a boil over medium heat, stirring a few times. Reduce the heat to low. Cover the saucepan and simmer 5 to 6 minutes, stirring a few times to release any bits of agar that may be stuck on the bottom of the pan. Check a spoonful of liquid for specks of agar. There should be none or very little. Cover and simmer on lowest heat if necessary, until the agar is completely dissolved.

Slowly whisk the coconut milk into the dissolved agar. Add the cinnamon and salt and cook covered over low heat for 2 minutes, raising the lid and whisking a few times. Raise the heat slightly and bring to a gentle boil.

Reduce the heat to the lowest simmer and add the sugar. Simmer for 1 minute. Add the cocoa powder 1 tablespoon at a time, whisking until dissolved after each addition. Simmer covered for 4 minutes. Add the maple syrup and reduce the heat as needed to allow the mixture to cook at a low boil for 1 minute. Add the chocolate and stir until it has melted.
Remove the saucepan from the heat and add the vanilla extract.

Test the texture of the panna cotta: Spoon a scant tablespoon of the liquid panna cotta into a small cup and refrigerate for 10 minutes. Keep the saucepan covered, but whisk the mixture a few times while the test is chilling. If the panna cotta tastes too firm, add a small amount of water (as little as 1 tablespoon, or more as needed) and repeat the test until you are satisfied with the texture.

Pour the panna cotta into the ramekins. Refrigerate the ramekins for 15 to 30 minutes, until the panna cotta is set, or up to a day ahead of time. (Panna cotta does not have to be served molded. Pour the still-liquid panna cotta into wine glasses and top with All-Purpose Chocolate Syrup and berries.)
